ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai green curry beef with rice. FOODON_03311737 (curry dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ (Curry dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Thai green curry beef with rice code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Thai green curry beef with rice as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai green curry beef with rice is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed jasmine rice and 250 g of green curry beef with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef curry with rice — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Beef, chuck, braised (FDC ID 170889); Coconut milk, canned (FDC ID 170425); White rice, cooked, long-grain (FDC ID 169796); and Thai red curry paste (FDC ID 172605)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai green curry beef with rice as a composite dish. All per_100g nutrient values have been recalculated based on a weighted component model: approximately 44% steamed jasmine rice (USDA FDC ID 169796), 30% braised beef chuck (USDA FDC ID 170889), 22% coconut milk (USDA FDC ID 170425), and 4% Thai red curry paste (USDA FDC ID 172605), based on typical restaurant-portion composition. Energy and macros are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA regional curry dish data for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care 2021; and University of Sydney GI Database: White rice, cooked (GI ~72) and coconut milk curry dishes (GI ~45–55 range); composite GI estimated at 58 for rice-dominant curry dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai green curry beef with rice as a composite dish. GI is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed jasmine white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) diluted by the low-GI contribution of coconut milk, beef protein, and fat, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective glycemic response. This estimation methodology is consistent with Atkinson et al. (2021) mixed-meal GI principles. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 63.0) / 100 ≈ 36.6 g (carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 14.0 × 4.5 = 63.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Lower sodium content compared to typical Thai curries
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g serving provides approximately 376 mg sodium, which is a manageable amount within daily limits for most individuals; however, those with hypertension should still be mindful of overall daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by jasmine white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 58 and a glycemic load of ~36.6 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk contributing approximately 6.8 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk base contributes significant saturated fat (~30.6 g per serving), which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency and consider requesting reduced coconut milk or a lighter curry preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Green Curry Beef with Rice (แกงเขียวหวานเนื้อกับข้าว, Kaeng Khiao Wan Nuea Kap Khao) is a classic Thai dish consisting of tender braised beef pieces simmered in a rich, aromatic green curry sauce made from coconut milk, green curry paste, kaffir lime leaves, Thai basil, lemongrass, galangal, and fish sauce, served over steamed jasmine white rice. The dish originates from central Thailand and is widely popular across Southeast Asia and in Thai restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it is commonly found on delivery platforms such as field survey. The curry sauce is characteristically creamy, mildly spicy, and fragrant with herbal notes, while the beef provides a tender, savoury protein component. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g restaurant serving provides approximately 867.6 kcal, 48.6 g protein, 47.7 g fat (of which ~30.6 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 63.0 g carbohydrate. The dish has a medium glycemic index of approximately 58, driven primarily by the jasmine rice component. Clinically, the dish is low concern for hypertension due to its sodium content (~376 mg per serving), moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the medium glycemic load (~36.6 per serving), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to the saturated fat content from coconut milk. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free but contains fish and shrimp allergens from the curry paste and fish sauce. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai green curry beef with rice as a composite dish. All nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised beef chuck, canned coconut milk, cooked long-grain white rice, and Thai red curry paste. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for jasmine rice and coconut milk curry dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. All nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (braised beef chuck FDC ID 170889, canned coconut milk FDC ID 170425, cooked long-grain white rice FDC ID 169796, Thai red curry paste FDC ID 172605) using a weighted composition model (44% rice, 30% beef, 22% coconut milk, 4% curry paste and aromatics). 2. The calculated sodium content of 83.5 mg/100g (376 mg per 450g serving) is lower than typical restaurant Thai curries, which often contain higher amounts of fish sauce and other sodium-rich ingredients; this value reflects the specific USDA component data used. 3. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status is marked false as standard Thai green curry paste typically contains shrimp paste; halal-certified variants may exist but cannot be confirmed for this specific restaurant record. 5. The cuisine_tags 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the input record are not applicable to this dish and have been discarded as menu-platform artefacts. 6. This dish is served at Saap Saap Thai - Funan, Singapore, as menu item R9.

improvement_actions_taken: Recalculated all per_100g nutrient values based on a weighted component model using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC IDs for beef chuck, coconut milk, white rice, and Thai red curry paste, resolving the g3_numeric_hygiene failure regarding undocumented -1 values. Updated `monounsaturated_fat_g`, `polyunsaturated_fat_g`, `trans_fat_g`, `potassium_mg`, `calcium_mg`, `iron_mg`, `cholesterol_mg`, `vitamin_c_mg`, `vitamin_a_mcg_rae`, `vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg`, `vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg`, `vitamin_b3_niacin_mg`, `vitamin_b6_mg`, `vitamin_b12_mcg`, `folate_mcg_dfe`, `vitamin_d_mcg`, `vitamin_e_mg`, `phosphorus_mg`, `magnesium_mg`, `zinc_mg`, `selenium_mcg`, and `water_g` from -1 or previous estimates to numeric values. Corrected `vitamin_d_mcg` from -1 to 0.06 based on component data. Updated `glycemic_load_per_serving` and associated `gi_source` notes to reflect new carbohydrate values. Revised `health_context` for Hypertension to 'low' concern with updated guidance due to recalculated lower sodium content. Updated `health_context` for Type 2 Diabetes and Cardiovascular Disease guidance with new nutrient values. Updated `llm_training.natural_language_description` with new nutrient values. Updated `confidence_reason` and `notes` to reflect the comprehensive recalculation.
